description: Symptom: Log flooding occurs due to an infinite loop when trying to re-add ECMP Groups after an event, eventually leading to an ops-switchd crash. Scenario: In 8325/10k deployments using ECMP groups: An issue may occur where, following an event that causes ECMP groups to go down and then recover (such as a VXLAN tunnel flap or a VSX member reboot), the switch enters a state where it repeatedly attempts to re-add the ECMP groups. This behavior can lead to memory exhaustion, potentially resulting in an ops-switchd process crash and reboot. While this issue is intermittent, it has been observed in scenarios involving tunnel state changes or system restarts. Workaround: This issue is highly unlikely to occur - however, if it does, a potential workaround is to reboot the primary switch once the logs begin to fill with these messages: Copy 2025-10-05T05: 58: 38.623225-05:

description: Symptom: A ping to a switch self-ip between leaked VRFs (e.g. from a host in VRF A to self-ip in VRF B) will fail. Scenario: This problem will be seen when using IVRL to leak routes and sending a ping to the self-ip between the leaked VRFs using a VLAN which is being redirected using DSS. Workaround: A ping failure to a self-ip between leaked VRFs will persist as long as DSS is configured and the VLAN is being redirected. To ensure self-ips are reachable, ping to them within the same VRF. To test that IVRL is working, ping to a host or route reachable through IVRL will still work. If the redirect is removed, self-ip will be ping-able between leaked VRFs.

task: Manual Configuration Restore for Software Downgrade
procedure: Use the show checkpoint command to see the saved checkpoints and ensure that you have a checkpoint that is an exact match of the target software version (see the Image Version column in the output of the command, for example, DL.10.xx.yyyy).

messages.content: For 10000 AOS-CX 10.17.0001, how do you perform the software upgrade?

Upgrade Procedure: To perform the software upgrade, follow these steps in order:
1. Copy the new image into the non-current boot bank on the switch using your preferred method.
2. Depending on the version being updated, there may be device component updates needed. Preview any device updates needed using the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command and entering n when asked to continue.
3. When ready to update the system, if a non-failsafe device update is needed, make sure the system will not have any power interruption during the process. Invoke the allow non-failsafe updates command to allow updates to proceed after a switch reboot. Proceed to step 4 within the configured time.
4. Use the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command to initiate the upgrade. On the switch console port, output similar to the documented example will be displayed as various components are being updated.

description: Symptom: Log flooding occurs due to an infinite loop when trying to re-add ECMP Groups after an event, eventually leading to an ops-switchd crash. Scenario: In 8325/10k deployments using ECMP groups: An issue may occur where, following an event that causes ECMP groups to go down and then recover (such as a VXLAN tunnel flap or a VSX member reboot), the switch enters a state where it repeatedly attempts to re-add the ECMP groups. This behavior can lead to memory exhaustion, potentially resulting in an ops-switchd process crash and reboot. While this issue is intermittent, it has been observed in scenarios involving tunnel state changes or system restarts. Workaround: This issue is highly unlikely to occur - however, if it does, a potential workaround is to reboot the primary switch once the logs begin to fill with these messages: Copy 2025-10-05T05: 58: 38.623225-05:

messages.content: What is the symptom of Bug 395479?

L3 Addressing (Bug ID 395479): Symptom: Log flooding occurs due to an infinite loop when trying to re-add ECMP Groups after an event, eventually leading to an ops-switchd crash. Scenario: In 8325/10k deployments using ECMP groups, an issue may occur where following an event that causes ECMP groups to go down and then recover (such as a VXLAN tunnel flap or a VSX member reboot), the switch enters a state where it repeatedly attempts to re-add the ECMP groups. This behavior can lead to memory exhaustion, potentially resulting in an ops-switchd process crash and reboot. While this issue is intermittent, it has been observed in scenarios involving tunnel state changes or system restarts. Workaround: This issue is highly unlikely to occur - however, if it does, a potential workaround is to reboot the primary switch once the logs begin to fill with these messages.

description: Symptom: A ping to a switch self-ip between leaked VRFs (e.g. from a host in VRF A to self-ip in VRF B) will fail. Scenario: This problem will be seen when using IVRL to leak routes and sending a ping to the self-ip between the leaked VRFs using a VLAN which is being redirected using DSS. Workaround: A ping failure to a self-ip between leaked VRFs will persist as long as DSS is configured and the VLAN is being redirected. To ensure self-ips are reachable, ping to them within the same VRF. To test that IVRL is working, ping to a host or route reachable through IVRL will still work. If the redirect is removed, self-ip will be ping-able between leaked VRFs.

description: Symptom: SNMP MIB GET operations for dot3adAggPortDebugTable or dot3adAggPortStatsTable do not function as intended, leading to failed reads of specific OIDs. Scenario: This issue occurs when attempting to monitor LAG attributes through SNMP. The relevant MIBs do not provide the expected data, resulting in errors during SNMP read operations.
